solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \vec{r}=\vec{q}+\vec{p} \\ & |\vec{r}|=\sqrt{q^2+p^2+2 p q \cos \theta} \\ & |r|^2=4+12+2 \times \sqrt{3} \times 2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & |r|^2=24 \\ & \left|\vec{p} \times\left(\vec{q}-3(\vec{q}-\vec{p})^2\right)\right|^2+3|\vec{r}|^2=|-2 \vec{p} \times \vec{q}|^2+3|\vec{r}|^2 \\ & =4 \times|\vec{p}||\vec{q}|^2 \sin ^2 \theta+3 \times 24=4 \times 12 \times 4 \times\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)+72=128+72=200 \end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Evening Shift

question:
Let $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(5,4,2)$ on the line $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$.

Then the length of the projection of the vector $\alpha \hat{i}+\beta \hat{j}+\gamma \hat{k}$ on the vector $6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ is :
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{3}=\frac{z-1}{-1}=\lambda \\ & (2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda+3,1-\lambda) \\ & \overrightarrow{P F}=(2 \lambda-6) \hat{i}+(3 \lambda-1) \hat{j}+(-\lambda-1) \hat{k} \\ & \overrightarrow{P F} \cdot(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})=0 \\ & 4 \lambda-12+9 \lambda-3+\lambda+1=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=1 \\ & \Rightarrow F \equiv(1,6,0) \\ & \Rightarrow \text { length of projection of } \hat{i}+6 \hat{j} \text { on } 6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ & =\left|\frac{(\hat{i}+6 \hat{j}) \cdot(6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})}{|6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}|}\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{7}(6+12)=\frac{18}{7} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Option (3) is correct. } \end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Morning Shift

question: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}=8 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ be a vector such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$. If $\vec{c} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=4$, then $|\vec{a}+\vec{c}|^2$ is equal to :
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Let } \vec{c}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}\\ &\begin{aligned} & \vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{b}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & k \\ -1 & 2 & 2 \\ x & y & z \end{array}\right| \\ & =(2 z-2 y) \hat{i}+(z+2 x) \hat{j}+(-y-2 x) \hat{k} \\ & \equiv 8 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & 2 z-2 y=8 \\ & \left.\begin{array}{l} z+2 x=7 \\ -y-2 x=3 \end{array}\right\} y=3-2 x, z=7-2 x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \vec{c} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=4 \\ & x+y+z=4 \\ & \text { Now } x+3-2 x+7-2 x=4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=2 \\ & \quad y=-1 \\ & \quad z=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \vec{c}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ & |\vec{a}+\vec{c}|^2=|\hat{i}+\hat{j}+5 \hat{k}|^2 \\ & =27 \end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Morning Shift

question: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$ be vectors such that $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|=\sqrt{29}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$. If $\lambda_1, \lambda_2\left(\lambda_1>\lambda_2\right)$ are the possible values of $(\vec{c}+\vec{d}) \cdot(-7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$, then the equation $\mathrm{K}^2 x^2+\left(\mathrm{K}^2-5 \mathrm{~K}+\lambda_1\right) x y+\left(3 \mathrm{~K}+\frac{\lambda_2}{2}\right) y^2-8 x+12 y+\lambda_2=0$ represents a circle, for K equal to :
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & |\vec{c}+\vec{d}|=\sqrt{29} \\ & \vec{c} \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})-(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \times \vec{d}=0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & (\vec{c}+\vec{d}) \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \vec{c}+\vec{d}=\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{29}=|\lambda| \sqrt{29} \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 1 \Rightarrow \vec{c}+\vec{d}= \pm(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \\ & \Rightarrow(\vec{c}+\vec{d}) \cdot(-7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})= \pm(-14+6+12)= \pm 4 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda_1=4, \lambda_2=-4 \end{aligned} $$

Given equation represents a circle

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow K^2=3 K+\frac{\lambda_2}{2} \text { and } K^2-5 K+\lambda_1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow K^2=3 K-2 \text { and } K^2-5 K+4=0 \\ & \Rightarrow K=1,2 \text { and } K=4,1 \\ & \Rightarrow K=1 \end{aligned} $$

⇒ option (4) is correct.
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Morning Shift

question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\vec{b}=\lambda \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \lambda \in \boldsymbol{Z}$ be two vectors. Let $\vec{c}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ and $\vec{d}$ be a vector of magnitude 2 in $y z$-plane. If $|\vec{c}|=\sqrt{53}$, then the maximum possible value of $(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d})^2$ is equal to :
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \bar{c}=\bar{a} \times \bar{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \bar{i} & \bar{j} & \bar{k} \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & \lambda & 2 \end{array}\right|=(2-\lambda) \bar{i}-\bar{j}(4)+(2 \lambda) \bar{k} \\ & |\bar{c}|^2=53 \Rightarrow(2-\lambda)^2+16+(2 \lambda)^2=53=5 \lambda^2-4 \lambda-33=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 \lambda^2-15 \lambda+11 \lambda-33=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 \lambda(\lambda-3)+11(\lambda-3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\lambda-3)(5 \lambda+11)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=3, \lambda \neq-\frac{11}{5} \,\,\,\,\,(\because \lambda \in Z)\\ & \therefore \bar{c}=(-1,-4,6) \end{aligned} $$

Let $\therefore \bar{d}=(0, y, z)$

Given $|\bar{d}|=2 \Rightarrow y^2+z^2=4$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \bar{c} . \bar{d}=0-4 y+6 z \\ & (\bar{c} . \bar{d})^2=(-4 y+6 z)^2 \\ & =16 y^2+36 z^2-48 y z \\ & =16 y^2+36\left(4-y^2\right)-48 y \cdot \sqrt{4-y^2} \end{aligned} $$

Let $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{y})=-20 y^2+144-48 y \sqrt{4-y^2}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & f^1(y)=-40 y-48\left(\sqrt{4-y^2}\right)-48 y\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{4-y^2}}\right)(-2 y) \\ & =-40 y-48\left(\frac{4-y^2-y^2}{\sqrt{4-y^2}}\right) \\ & f^1(y)=0 \Leftarrow 40 y=96\left(\frac{y^2-2}{\sqrt{4-y^2}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 5 y \sqrt{4-y^2}=12\left(y^2-2\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 25 y^2\left(4-y^2\right)=144\left(y^4+4-4 y^2\right) \\ & =169 y^4-676 y^2+576=0 \\ & =169 y^4-208 y^2-468 y^2+576=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 13 y^2\left(13 y^2-16\right)-36\left(13 y^2-16\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2=\frac{16}{13}(\text { or }) \frac{36}{13} \\ & \Rightarrow y= \pm \frac{4}{\sqrt{13}}(\text { or }) \pm \frac{6}{\sqrt{13}} \\ & \therefore \text { Maximum of }(\bar{c} . \bar{d})^2=208 \end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Evening Shift

question: Let a vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\sqrt{2} \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\lambda \hat{k}, \lambda>0$, make an obtuse angle with the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=-\lambda^2 \hat{i}+4 \sqrt{2} \hat{j}+4 \sqrt{2} \hat{k}$ and an angle $\theta, \frac{\pi}{6}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}$, with the positive $z$-axis. If the set of all possible values of $\lambda$ is $(\alpha, \beta)-\{\gamma\}$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \vec{a}^{\wedge} \hat{k} \in\left(\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{\left(2+1+\lambda^2\right)}} \in\left(0, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \Rightarrow 0<\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{3+\lambda^2}}<\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \lambda>0 \& \lambda \in(-3,3) \Rightarrow \lambda \in(0,3) \ldots \ldots \ldots .(1) \end{aligned} $$

$\vec{a}^{\wedge} \vec{b}$ is obtuse

$$ \begin{aligned} & \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}<0 \Rightarrow-\sqrt{2} \lambda^2-4 \sqrt{2}+4 \sqrt{2} \lambda<0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda^2-4 \lambda+4>0 \Rightarrow(\lambda-2)^2>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda \in R-\{2\} \ldots \ldots .(2) \end{aligned} $$

(1) \& (2) ⇒ $\lambda \in(0,3)-\{2\} \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta+\gamma=5$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Evening Shift

question: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\lambda \hat{k}, \lambda \in \mathbb{R}$. Let the projection of the vector $\vec{v}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ on the diagonal $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}$ of the parallelogram ABCD be of length one unit. If $\alpha, \beta$, where $\alpha>\beta$, be the roots of the equation $\lambda^2 x^2-6 \lambda x+5=0$, then $2 \alpha-\beta$ is equal to
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{A B}=2 \bar{i}+4 \bar{j}-5 \bar{k} \\ & \overline{A D}=\bar{i}+2 \bar{j}+\lambda \bar{k} \\ & \bar{V}=\bar{i}+\bar{j}+\bar{k} \\ & \bar{V} \cdot \overline{A C} \\ & \frac{|\overline{A C}|}{} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \overline{A C}=\overline{A B}+\overline{B C} \\ & 18 \lambda=54 \\ & 18 \lambda=54 \end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 22nd January Morning Shift

question: Let $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ be three vectors such that $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})$. If $|\vec{a}|=1,|\vec{b}|=4,|\vec{c}|=2$, and the angle between $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ is $60^{\circ}$, then $|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}|$ is equal to
solution:
Given that

$$ \begin{aligned} & \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c}) \\ & \vec{a} \times \vec{b}-2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})=0 \\ & \vec{a} \times(\vec{b}-2 \vec{c})=0 \end{aligned} $$

This means $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}-2 \vec{c}$ are parallel vectors

$$ \begin{aligned} & \vec{b}-2 \vec{c}=\lambda \vec{a} \\ & \Rightarrow \vec{b}=\lambda \vec{a}+2 \vec{c}-(1) \end{aligned} $$

It is given that $|\vec{a}|=1,|b|=4$ and $|\vec{c}|=2$, angle between $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}, \theta=60^{\circ}$.

$$ |b|^2=|\lambda \vec{a}+2 \vec{c}|^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|b|^2=\lambda^2|a|^2+4|c|^2+4 \lambda(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $16=\lambda^2(1)^2+4(2)^2+4 \lambda(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\lambda^2+4 \lambda(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})=0$ .......(2)

Taking dot product with $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ in equation (1)

$$ \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=(\lambda \vec{a}+2 \vec{c}) \cdot \vec{c} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|\vec{b}||\vec{c}| \cos 60^{\circ}=\lambda(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})+2|\vec{c}|^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4 \times 2 \times 1 / 2=\lambda(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})+2(2)^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4=\lambda(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})+8$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \lambda(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})=-4 $$

From (2) and (3)

$\lambda^2+4(-4)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\lambda^2-16=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \lambda= \pm 4 $$

from (3)

$$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=-4 / \lambda $$

if $\lambda=4$, then $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=-1$

if $\lambda=-4$, then $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=1$

$$ |\vec{a} \cdot c|=1 $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Evening Shift

question: Let $\vec{a}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{b}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{c}=\lambda \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{v}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$. If $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{c}=11$ and the length of the projection of $\vec{b}$ on $\vec{c}$ is $p$, then $9 p^2$ is equal to
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Given }\\ &\begin{gathered} \vec{a}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ \vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k} \\ \vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k} \\ \vec{v}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b} \\ \vec{v}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \end{array}\right| \\ \vec{v}=\hat{i}(2-3)-\hat{j}(-1-6)+\hat{k}(1+4) \\ \vec{v}=-\hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k} \\ \vec{v} \cdot \vec{c}=11 \end{gathered} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{gathered} (-\hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) \cdot(\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=11 \\ -\lambda+7+5=11 \\ -\lambda+12=11 \\ \lambda=1 \\ \vec{c}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k} \\ p=\frac{|\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}|}{|\vec{c}|} \\ \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \\ =2+1-1=2 \\ |\vec{c}|=\sqrt{1^2+1^2+1^2}=\sqrt{3} \\ p=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \\ p^2=\frac{4}{3} \\ 9 p^2=12 \end{gathered} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Evening Shift

question: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}$. Then $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{d}$ is equal to :
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & (\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{d}=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d}-\vec{b} \cdot \vec{d} \\ & \because \vec{d}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a} \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{d}=[\vec{a} \vec{c} \vec{a}]=0 \\ & \text { And } \vec{b} \cdot \vec{d}=\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{c} \times \vec{a}) \\ & =\vec{b} \cdot((\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times \vec{a}) \\ & \Rightarrow(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{d}=\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{a} \times(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})) \\ & =\vec{b} \cdot[\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} \vec{a}-\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a} \vec{b}] \\ & =(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^2-(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a})(\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}) \\ & =(-8)^2-6 \times 11=64-66=-2 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Option (2) is correct. } \end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 23rd January Morning Shift

question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{c}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. Let $\vec{v}$ be the vector in the plane of the vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, such that the length of its projection on the vector $\vec{c}$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}$. Then $|\vec{v}|$ is equal to
solution:
Given

$$ a=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}, b=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}, \text { and } c=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k} . $$

$v$ is in the plane of vectors $a$ and $b$,

$$ \text { so } v=a+\lambda b=(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+(-1+3 \lambda) \hat{j}+(-1-\lambda) \hat{k} \text {. } $$

It is given that length of projection of $v$ on $c$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left|\frac{v \cdot c}{|c|}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \\ & |v \cdot c|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \times|c| \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \quad=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \times \sqrt{(2)^2+(1)^2+(3)^2} \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \times \sqrt{14}=1 . \\ & |((2+\lambda) \hat{i}+(-1+3 \lambda) \hat{j}+(-1-\lambda) \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})|=1, \\ & |4+2 \lambda-1+3 \lambda-3-3 \lambda|=1, \\ & |2 \lambda|=1 \Longrightarrow \lambda= \pm \frac{1}{2} \Longrightarrow \lambda^2=\frac{1}{4} . \\ & |v|^2=|a+\lambda b|^2, \\ & |v|^2=|a|^2+\lambda^2|b|^2+2 \lambda(a \cdot b) .\{a \cdot b=0\} \\ & =6+11 \lambda^2+0 . \\ & |v|=\sqrt{6+11 \lambda^2} . \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Put value } \lambda^2=\frac{1}{4} \text { : }\\ &\begin{aligned} & |v|=\sqrt{6+\frac{11}{4}}, \\ & |v|=\sqrt{\frac{24+11}{4}}, \\ & |v|=\sqrt{\frac{35}{4}} \\ & |v|=\frac{\sqrt{35}}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Evening Shift

question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\vec{c}$ is a vector such that $2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97$, then $|\vec{c} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^2$ is equal to
solution:
Given

$$ \begin{gathered} \vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}, \quad \vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ a(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=0 \\ 2 \vec{a} \times \vec{c}+3 \vec{b} \times \vec{c}=0 \\ (2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}) \times \vec{c}=0 \end{gathered} $$

This means $2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ are parallel.

$$ \Rightarrow \vec{c}=\lambda(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}) $$

$$ \vec{c}=\lambda[2(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+3(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})] $$

$$ \vec{c}=\lambda(7 \hat{i}-13 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k}) $$

Given

$$ (\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97 $$

$$ \begin{gathered} \vec{a}-\vec{b}=(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})-(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \\ =\hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{gathered} $$

Put in equation:

$$ \begin{gathered} (\hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot \lambda(7 \hat{i}-13 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k})=-97 \\ \lambda(7+52+38)=-97 \\ \lambda(97)=-97 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \\ \vec{c}=-1(7 \hat{i}-13 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k}) \\ \vec{c}=-7 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-19 \hat{k} \\ \vec{c} \times \hat{k}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -7 & 13 & -19 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\ =\hat{i}(13)-\hat{j}(-7)+\hat{k}(0) \\ =13 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j} \\ |\vec{c} \times \hat{k}|^2=13^2+7^2=169+49=218 \end{gathered} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Evening Shift

question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ and $\vec{c}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$. Let $\vec{d}$ be a vector such that $|\vec{d}-\vec{a}|=\sqrt{11},|\vec{c} \times \vec{d}|=3$ and the angle between $\vec{c}$ and $\vec{d}$ is $\frac{\pi}{4}$. Then $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d}$ is equal to
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j} \\ & |\vec{a}|=\sqrt{4+1+4}=3,|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { It is given that } \vec{c}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}\\ &\begin{gathered} \vec{c}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right| \\ \vec{c}=\hat{i}(0+2)-\hat{j}(0+2)+\hat{k}(-2-1) \\ \vec{c}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \end{gathered} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &|\vec{c}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=\sqrt{9}=3\\ &\text { It is given that }|\vec{c} \times \vec{d}|=3 \& \text { angle between } \vec{c} \& \vec{d} \text { is } \pi / 4\\ &\begin{gathered} |\vec{c}||\vec{d}| \sin \frac{\pi}{4}=3 \\ 3|\vec{d}| \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=3 \Rightarrow|\vec{d}|=\sqrt{2} \\ |\vec{d}-\vec{a}|=\sqrt{11}(\text { given }) \\ |\vec{d}-\vec{a}|^2=11 \\ |\vec{d}|^2+|\vec{a}|^2-2 \vec{d} \cdot \vec{a}=11 \\ 2+9-2 \vec{d} \cdot \vec{a}=11 \text { use }|\vec{a}|=3 \\ 11-2 \vec{d} \cdot \vec{a}=11 \\ -2 \vec{d} \cdot \vec{a}=0 \\ \vec{d} \cdot \vec{a}=0 \end{gathered} \end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift

question: Let the lines $\mathrm{L}_1: \vec{r}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}), \lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\mathrm{L}_2: \vec{r}=(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\mu(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}), \mu \in \mathbb{R}$, intersect at the point R . Let P and Q be the points lying on lines $L_1$ and $L_2$, respectively, such that $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}|=\sqrt{29}$ and $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}|=\sqrt{\frac{47}{3}}$. If the point P lies in the first octant, then $27(\mathrm{QR})^2$ is equal to
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} L_1 & : \vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \\ L_2 & : \vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j})+\mu(5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}) \end{aligned} $$

at the intersection point R , position vectors are equal.

$$ (\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j})+\mu(5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}) $$

equating components of $\hat{i}, \hat{j}$ and $\hat{k}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & 1+2 \lambda=4+5 \mu \Longrightarrow 2 \lambda-5 \mu=3 \text {...... (1) } \\ & 2+3 \lambda=1+2 \mu \Longrightarrow 3 \lambda-2 \mu=-1 \text {.....(2) } \\ & 3+4 \lambda=\mu \Longrightarrow \mu-4 \lambda=3 ...... (3)\\ & \text { eqn (1) } \times 3 \text { - eqn (2) } \times 2 \\ & -15 \mu+4 \mu=9+2 \Longrightarrow-11 \mu=11 \Longrightarrow \mu=-1 \end{aligned} $$

substitute in equation (1)

$$ 2 \lambda-5(-1)=3 \Longrightarrow \lambda=\frac{3-5}{2}=-1 $$

Now, checking equation (3) $(-1)-4(-1)=3=$ R.H.S consistent.

Now finding point R using $\lambda=-1$ in $L_1$

$$ \begin{gathered} \overrightarrow{O R}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \\ \overrightarrow{O R}=-\hat{i}-\hat{i}-\hat{k} \end{gathered} $$

Point P lies on $L_1$ so its position vector is $\quad \overrightarrow{O P}=(1+2 \lambda) \hat{i}+(2+3 \lambda) \hat{j}+(3+4 \lambda) \hat{k}$ It is given that

$$ \begin{gathered} |\overrightarrow{P R}|=\sqrt{29} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{P R}|^2=29 \\ \overrightarrow{P R}=\overrightarrow{O R}-\overrightarrow{O P} \\ \overrightarrow{P R}=(-2-2 \lambda) \hat{i}+(-3-3 \lambda) \hat{j}+(-4-4 \lambda) \hat{k} \\ |\overrightarrow{P R}|^2=4(1+\lambda)^2+9(1+\lambda)^2+16(1+\lambda)^2=29 \\ 29(1+\lambda)^2=29 \Rightarrow(1+\lambda)^2=1 \Rightarrow \lambda=0,-2 \end{gathered} $$

point $P$ lies in first octant, means position vector of $P$ must be positive, so $\lambda=0$

$$ \overrightarrow{O P}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k} $$

similarly Q lies on $L_2$, so position vector of Q is

$$ \begin{gathered} \overrightarrow{O Q}=(4+5 \mu) \hat{i}+(1+2 \mu) \hat{j}+\mu \hat{k} \\ |\overrightarrow{P Q}|=\sqrt{\frac{47}{3}} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{P Q}|^2=\frac{47}{3} \quad \text { (given) } \\ \overrightarrow{P Q}=\overrightarrow{O Q}-\overrightarrow{O P} \\ \overrightarrow{P Q}=(3+5 \mu) \hat{i}+(-1+2 \mu) \hat{j}+(\mu-3) \hat{k} \\ (3+5 \mu)^2+(-1+2 \mu)^2+(\mu-3)^2=\frac{47}{3} \\ 30 \mu^2+20 \mu+19=\frac{47}{3} \\ 90 \mu^2+60 \mu+57=47 \\ 90 \mu^2+60 \mu+10=0 \\ 9 \mu^2+6 \mu+1=0 \\ (3 \mu+1)^2=0 \Rightarrow \mu=-\frac{1}{3} \end{gathered} $$

finding position vector of Q using $\mu=-1 / 3$

$$ \begin{gathered} \overrightarrow{O Q}=\left(4-\frac{5}{3}\right) \hat{i}+\left(1-\frac{2}{3}\right) \hat{j}-\frac{1}{3} \hat{k} \\ \overrightarrow{O Q}=\frac{7}{3} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{3} \hat{j}-\frac{1}{3} \hat{k} \end{gathered} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { calculate } 27|Q R|^2 \\ & \qquad \overrightarrow{Q R}=\overrightarrow{O R}-\overrightarrow{O Q} \\ & \overrightarrow{Q R}=-\frac{10}{3} \hat{i}-\frac{4}{3} \hat{j}-\frac{2}{3} \hat{k} \\ & |\overrightarrow{Q R}|^2=\frac{9}{100}+\frac{9}{16}+\frac{9}{4}=\frac{9}{120} \\ & 27|\overrightarrow{Q R}|^2=\frac{9}{120} \times 27=360\end{aligned} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift

question: Let P be a point in the plane of the vectors $\overrightarrow{AB}=3\hat{i} + \hat{j} - \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{AC}=\hat{i} - \hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$ such that P is equidistant from the lines AB and AC. If $|\overrightarrow{AP}| = \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$, then the area of the triangle ABP is :
solution:
Point $P$ is equidistant from the lines $A B$ and $A C$. Geometrically, this means point $P$ must lie on the angle bisector of the angle formed by vectors $A B$ and $A C$.

First, we find the unit vectors along $A B$ and $A C$ :

  • $|A B|=\sqrt{3^2+1^2+(-1)^2}=\sqrt{11}$

  • $|A C|=\sqrt{1^2+(-1)^2+3^2}=\sqrt{11}$

Since the magnitudes are equal, the angle bisector $v$ is simply the sum of the two vectors:

$$ \begin{array}{r} v=A B+A C=(3+1) \hat{i}+(1-1) \hat{j}+(-1+3) \hat{k}=4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k} \end{array} $$

The vector $A P$ will be in the direction of the unit vector $\hat{v}$ :

$$ \hat{v}=\frac{4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{4^2+2^2}}=\frac{4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{20}}=\frac{4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}}{2 \sqrt{5}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{5}} $$

Determine Vector $A P$

Given that $|A P|=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$, we can write:

$$ A P=|A P| \hat{v}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}\left(\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{5}}\right)=\frac{1}{2}(2 \hat{i}+\hat{k})=\hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{k} $$

  1. Calculate the Area of Triangle $A B P$

The area of a triangle formed by two vectors $A B$ and $A P$ is given by the formula:

$$ \text { Area }=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A P}| $$

First, compute the cross product $A B \times A P$ :

$$ \begin{gathered} \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A P}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & \frac{1}{2} \end{array}\right| \\ =\hat{i}\left(\frac{1}{2}-0\right)-\hat{j}\left(\frac{3}{2}-(-1)\right)+\hat{k}(0-1) \\ =\frac{1}{2} \hat{i}-\frac{5}{2} \hat{j}-\hat{k} \\ |\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A P}|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\left(-\frac{5}{2}\right)^2+(-1)^2}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}+\frac{25}{4}+1}=\sqrt{\frac{26}{4}+\frac{4}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{30}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{30}}{2} \end{gathered} $$

Finally, the area of the triangle is:

Area $=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sqrt{30}}{2}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{30}}{4} $
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift

question: If the distance of the point $P(43, \alpha, \beta)$, $\beta < 0$, from the line $\vec{r} = 4\hat{i} - \hat{k} + \mu (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{k}), \mu \in \mathbb{R}$ along a line with direction ratios $3, -1, 0$ is $13\sqrt{10}$, then $\alpha^2 + \beta^2$ is equal to ________
solution:
• Point $P$ : $(43, \alpha, \beta)$ with the constraint $\beta<0$.

  • Target Line ( $L_1$ ) : Given by $\vec{r}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{k}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k})$.

    A general point $Q$ on this line is $(4+2 \mu, 0,-1+3 \mu)$.

  • Direction of measurement : The distance is measured along a line with direction ratios $(3,-1,0)$.

Find Point $Q$ on the Target Line

Since the distance is measured along the direction $d=(3,-1,0)$, the vector $P Q$ must be parallel to $d$.

$$ \begin{gathered} P Q=Q-P=(4+2 \mu-43, \quad 0-\alpha, \quad-1+3 \mu-\beta) \\ P Q=(2 \mu-39, \quad-\alpha, \quad 3 \mu-\beta-1) \\ P Q=(2 \mu-39, \quad-\alpha, \quad 3 \mu-\beta-1) \end{gathered} $$

Because $P Q$ is parallel to $(3,-1,0)$, their components must be proportional :

  1. 1.$\frac{2 \mu-39}{3}=\frac{-\alpha}{-1} \Longrightarrow 2 \mu-39=3 \alpha \Longrightarrow 2 \mu=3 \alpha+39$
  1. 2.$\frac{3 \mu-\beta-1}{0}$ suggests the numerator must be zero for a finite ratio to exist: $3 \mu-\beta-1= 0 \Longrightarrow \beta=3 \mu-1$

Calculate the Distance $P Q$

The distance $P Q$ is given as $13 \sqrt{10}$. Using the distance formula on the proportional components where the ratio is $k$ :

$$ \begin{gathered} P Q=k(3,-1,0)=(3 k,-k, 0) \\ |P Q|=\sqrt{(3 k)^2+(-k)^2+0^2}=\sqrt{10 k^2}=|k| \sqrt{10} \end{gathered} $$

Given $|P Q|=13 \sqrt{10}$, we find $|k|=13$, so $k= \pm 13$.

Solve for $\alpha, \mu$, and $\beta$

From our proportionality equations :

  • $-\alpha=-k \Longrightarrow \alpha=k$

  • $2 \mu-39=3 k$

Case 1 : $k=13$

  • $\alpha=13$

  • $2 \mu-39=3(13)=39 \Longrightarrow 2 \mu=78 \Longrightarrow \mu=39$

  • $\beta=3 \mu-1=3(39)-1=116$

  • Constraint Check : $\beta<0$ is False. (Reject this case)

Case 2 : $k=-13$

  • $\alpha=-13$

  • $2 \mu-39=3(-13)=-39 \Longrightarrow 2 \mu=0 \Longrightarrow \mu=0$

  • $\beta=3(0)-1=-1$

  • Constraint Check : $\beta<0$ is True. (Accept this case)

$\alpha^2+\beta^2=(-13)^2+(-1)^2=169+1=170$

The value of $\alpha^2+\beta^2$ is 170 .
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift

question:
For three unit vectors $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ satisfying

$$ |\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^2+|\vec{b}-\vec{c}|^2+|\vec{c}-\vec{a}|^2=9 \text { and }|2 \vec{a}+k \vec{b}+k \vec{c}|=3 \text {, } $$

the positive value of k is :
solution:
Given, $|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=1$

$|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^2+|\vec{b}-\vec{c}|^2+|\vec{c}-\vec{a}|^2=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{b}|^2-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+|\vec{b}|^2+|\vec{c}|^2-2 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+|\vec{c}|^2+|\vec{a}|^2-2 \vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $6+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=9 \quad\{|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=1\}$

$$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=-\frac{3}{2} $$

also,

$$ |\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^2=3+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}) $$

$=3+2\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right)=3-3=0$

So

$$ \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\vec{a}+\vec{b}=-\vec{c}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|^2=|\vec{c}|^2=1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{b}|^2+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $1+1+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=-\frac{1}{2} $$

Similarly,

$$ \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=-\frac{1}{2} \quad \text { and } \quad \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=-\frac{1}{2} $$

so,

$$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=-\frac{1}{2} $$

It is given that

$$ |2 \vec{a}+k \vec{b}+k \vec{c}|=3 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|2 \vec{a}+k \vec{b}+k \vec{c}|^2=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4|\vec{a}|^2+k^2|\vec{b}|^2+k^2|\vec{c}|^2+4 k \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+2 k^2 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+4 k \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4(1)^2+k^2(1)^2+k^2(1)^2+4 k\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+2 k^2\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+4 k\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4+2 k^2-2 k-k^2-2 k=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $k^2-4 k-5=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $(k-5)(k+1)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $k=5,-1$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift

question: Let $P Q R$ be a triangle such that $\overrightarrow{P Q}=-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}=a \hat{\mathrm{i}}+b \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, a, b \in \mathrm{Z}$. Let S be the point on QR , which is equidistant from the lines PQ and PR . If $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}|=9$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$, then the value of $3 a-4 b$ is $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
We are given that $|\overrightarrow{P R}|=9$ Using the distance formula for the vector $P R=a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$ :

$$ \sqrt{a^2+b^2+(-4)^2}=9 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $a^2+b^2+16=81$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ a^2+b^2=65 $$

The problem states that point $S$ lies on $Q R$ and is equidistant from lines $P Q$ and $P R$. In a triangle, the locus of points equidistant from two sides is the angle bisector. Therefore, $\overrightarrow{P S}$ is the internal angle bisector of $\angle Q P R$.

For an angle bisector $\overrightarrow{P S}$, it must be collinear with the sum of the unit vectors along the adjacent sides :

$$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\lambda\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{P Q}}{|\overrightarrow{P Q}|}+\frac{\overrightarrow{P R}}{|\overrightarrow{P R}|}\right) $$

where $\lambda$ is a scalar constant.

First, calculate $|\overrightarrow{P Q}|$ :

$$ |\overrightarrow{P Q}|=\sqrt{(-2)^2+(-1)^2+2^2}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=3 $$

Now, set up the bisector equation :

$$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\lambda\left(\frac{-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}}{3}+\frac{a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}}{9}\right) $$

Multiply the first term by $\frac{3}{3}$ to get a common denominator:

$$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\frac{\lambda}{9}((-6+a) \hat{i}+(-3+b) \hat{j}+(6-4) \hat{k}) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\frac{\lambda}{9}((a-6) \hat{i}+(b-3) \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) $$

We are given

$$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} $$

Comparing the $\hat{k}$-components:

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{\lambda}{9}(2)=2 \Rightarrow \lambda=9 $$

Substitute $\lambda=9$ into other components:

For $\hat{i}$ :

$$ a-6=1 \Rightarrow a=7 $$

For $\hat{j}$ :

$$ b-3=-7 \Rightarrow b=-4 $$

Check against the constraint :

$$ a^2+b^2=7^2+(-4)^2=49+16=65 $$

The values are consistent. value of $3 a-4 b$ :

$$ 3 a-4 b=3(7)-4(-4) $$ $=21+16=\mathbf{3 7}$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift

question:
Let the vectors $\vec{a} = -\hat{i} + \hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$ and $\vec{b} = \hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + \hat{k}$. For some $\lambda, \mu \in \mathbb{R}$, let $\vec{c} = \lambda \vec{a} + \mu \vec{b}$.

If $\vec{c} \cdot (3\hat{i} - 6\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}) = 10$ and $\vec{c} \cdot (\hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}) = -2$, then $|\vec{c}|^2$ is equal to :
solution:
$$\vec{c}=\lambda \vec{a}+\mu \vec{b}$$

$$\vec{c}=(\mu-\lambda)\hat{i}+(\lambda+3\mu)\hat{j}+(3\lambda+\mu)\hat{k}$$

$$\vec{c}\cdot(3\hat{i}-6\hat{j}+2\hat{k})=10$$

$$3(\mu-\lambda)-6(\lambda+3\mu)+2(3\lambda+\mu)=10$$

$$-3\lambda-13\mu=10 \qquad \ldots (1)$$

$$\vec{c}\cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=-2$$

$$5\mu+3\lambda=-2 \qquad \ldots (2)$$

Solving (1) & (2)

$$\lambda=1,\ \mu=-1$$

$$\vec{c}=-2\hat{i}-2\hat{j}+2\hat{k}$$

$$|\vec{c}|^2=12$$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift

question: Two adjacent sides of a parallelogram PQRS are given by $\overrightarrow{PQ} = \hat{j} + \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{PS} = \hat{i} - \hat{j}$. If the side PS is rotated about the point P by an acute angle $\alpha$ in the plane of the parallelogram so that it becomes perpendicular to the side PQ, then $\sin^2\left(\frac{5\alpha}{2}\right) - \sin^2\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ is equal to:
solution:
Let angle between $\overrightarrow{PQ}$ & $\overrightarrow{PS}$ is $\theta$

$$\cos \theta = \frac{\overrightarrow{PQ} . \overrightarrow{PS}}{\left|\overrightarrow{PQ}\right|\left|\overrightarrow{PS}\right|}$$

$$\cos \theta = \frac{0-1+0}{\sqrt{2}\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\cos \theta = -\frac{1}{2}$$

$$\theta = \frac{2\pi}{3}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{2\pi}{3} - \frac{\pi}{2} \ \text{So} \ \alpha = \frac{\pi}{6}$$

$$\sin^2 \frac{5\alpha}{2} - \sin^2 \frac{\alpha}{2} = \sin\left(\frac{5\alpha}{2} + \frac{\alpha}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{5\alpha}{2} - \frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

$$= \sin \frac{6\alpha}{2} \times \sin \frac{4\alpha}{2}$$

$$= \sin 3\alpha \times \sin 2\alpha$$

$$= \sin \frac{\pi}{2} \times \sin \frac{\pi}{3}$$

$$= 1 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift

question: The velocity of a particle is given as $\vec{v} = -x \hat{i} + 2y \hat{j} - z \hat{k}$ m/s. The magnitude of acceleration at point (1, 2, 4) is ________ m/s2.
solution:
The acceleration vector $\vec{a}$ is the total derivative of velocity with respect to time:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$$

The velocity of the particle is given as $\vec{v} = -x\hat{i} + 2y\hat{j} - z\hat{k}\ \mathrm{m/s}$

The x-component of velocity is $v_x = -x$

So, the x-component of acceleration is $a_x = \frac{dv_x}{dt} = \frac{dv_x}{dx} \times \frac{dx}{dt} = v_x\frac{dv_x}{dx} = (-x)\frac{d(-x)}{dx} = x$

The y-component of velocity is $v_y = 2y$

So, the y-component of acceleration is $a_y = \frac{dv_y}{dt} = \frac{dv_y}{dy} \times \frac{dy}{dt} = v_y\frac{dv_y}{dx} = (2y)\frac{d(2y)}{dx} = (2y)(2) = 4y$

The z-component of velocity is $v_z = -z$

So, the z-component of acceleration is $a_z = \frac{dv_z}{dt} = \frac{dv_z}{dy} \times \frac{dz}{dt} = v_z\frac{dv_z}{dx} = (-z)\frac{d(-z)}{dx} = (-z)(-1) = z$

Thus, the acceleration vector is $\vec{a} = x\hat{i} + 4y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$.

At point $(1,2,4)$:

$$\vec{a} = (1)\hat{i} + 4(2)\hat{j} + (4)\hat{k} = \hat{i} + 8\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$$

The magnitude $|\vec{a}|$ is,

$$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$$

$$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{1^2 + 8^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 64 + 16} = \sqrt{81}$$

$$|\vec{a}| = 9\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

Therefore, the magnitude of acceleration at the given point is $9\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$. Therefore, the correct option is (B).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift

question: If $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are two vectors such that $|\vec{a}|=2$ and $|\vec{b}|=3$, then the maximum value of $3|(3 \vec{a}+2 \vec{b})|+4|(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})|$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}=3 \sqrt{9 a^2+4 b^2+12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}+4 \sqrt{9 a^2+4 b^2-12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}} \\ & =3 \sqrt{36+36+12 \times 6 \cos \theta}+4 \sqrt{36+36-72 \cos \theta} \\ & =3 \sqrt{72+72 \cos \theta}+4 \sqrt{72-72 \cos \theta} \\ & =18 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{1+\cos \theta}+24 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{1-\cos \theta} \\ & =18 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}+24 \sqrt{2}\left(\sqrt{2} \sin \frac{\theta}{2}\right) \\ & =36\left(\cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)+48 \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\max }=\sqrt{(36)^2+(48)^2}=\sqrt{6^4+6^2 \times 8^2}=60\end{aligned}$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift

question: Let $\hat{u}$ and $\hat{v}$ be unit vectors inclined at an acute angle such that $|\hat{u} \times \hat{v}|=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=\lambda \hat{u}+\hat{v}+(\hat{u} \times \hat{v})$, then $\lambda$ is equal to:
solution:
Since $\hat{u}$ and $\hat{v}$ are unit vectors,

$$ |\hat{u}\times \hat{v}|=|\hat{u}||\hat{v}|\sin\theta=\sin\theta $$

Given

$$ |\hat{u}\times \hat{v}|=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

So,

$$ \sin\theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

The angle is acute, hence

$$ \theta=60^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{u}\cdot \hat{v}=\cos 60^\circ=\frac{1}{2} $$

Now,

$$ \vec A=\lambda \hat{u}+\hat{v}+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v}) $$

We take dot product with $\hat{u}$:

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{u} = \lambda (\hat{u}\cdot \hat{u})+(\hat{v}\cdot \hat{u})+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{u} $$

Now,

$$ \hat{u}\cdot \hat{u}=1,\qquad \hat{v}\cdot \hat{u}=\frac{1}{2},\qquad (\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{u}=0 $$

Therefore,

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{u}=\lambda+\frac{1}{2} $$

So,

$$ \lambda=\vec A\cdot \hat{u}-\frac{1}{2} \qquad \text{(1)} $$

Now take dot product with $\hat{v}$:

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{v} = \lambda (\hat{u}\cdot \hat{v})+(\hat{v}\cdot \hat{v})+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{v} $$

Using

$$ \hat{u}\cdot \hat{v}=\frac{1}{2},\qquad \hat{v}\cdot \hat{v}=1,\qquad (\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{v}=0 $$

we get

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{v}=\frac{\lambda}{2}+1 $$

Hence,

$$ \frac{\lambda}{2}=\vec A\cdot \hat{v}-1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda=2(\vec A\cdot \hat{v})-2 \qquad \text{(2)} $$

Now let

$$ x=\vec A\cdot \hat{u}, \qquad y=\vec A\cdot \hat{v} $$

Then from above,

$$ x=\lambda+\frac{1}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad y=\frac{\lambda}{2}+1 $$

From the first,

$$ \lambda=x-\frac{1}{2} $$

Also,

$$ x-2y=\left(\lambda+\frac{1}{2}\right)-2\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}+1\right) =\lambda+\frac{1}{2}-\lambda-2=-\frac{3}{2} $$

So we form $\lambda$ as a linear combination of $x$ and $y$.

Check Option A:

$$ \frac{4}{3}x-\frac{2}{3}y = \frac{4}{3}\left(\lambda+\frac{1}{2}\right)-\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}+1\right) $$

$$ = \frac{4\lambda}{3}+\frac{2}{3}-\frac{\lambda}{3}-\frac{2}{3} = \lambda $$

Hence,

$$ \lambda=\frac{4}{3}(\vec A\cdot \hat{u})-\frac{2}{3}(\vec A\cdot \hat{v}) $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A}} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift

question: Let $O$ be the origin, $\overrightarrow{O P}=\vec{a}$ and $\overrightarrow{O Q}=\vec{b}$. If $R$ is the point on $\overrightarrow{O P}$ such that $\overrightarrow{O P}=5 \overrightarrow{O R}$, and $M$ is the point such that $\overrightarrow{O Q}=5 \overrightarrow{R M}$, then $\overrightarrow{P M}$ is equal to :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OR}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}}{5}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}}{5} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{RM}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OQ}}}{5}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}}{5} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OM}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OR}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}}{5} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OM}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\overline{\mathrm{b}}}{5} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PM}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OM}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}} \\ & \frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}}{5}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \\ & =\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}}{5}\end{aligned}$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift

question: Let $\vec{a}=\sqrt{7} \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$. If $\vec{r}$ is a vector such that $\vec{r} \times \vec{a}+\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{a}=0$, then $|3 \vec{r}|^2$ is equal to:
solution:
Given

$$ \vec{a}=\sqrt{7}\,\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \qquad \vec{b}=\hat{j}+2\hat{k} $$

and

$$ \vec{r}\times \vec{a}+\vec{a}\times \vec{b}=\vec{0}, \qquad \vec{r}\cdot \vec{a}=0 $$

We have to find $|3\vec{r}|^2$.

From

$$ \vec{r}\times \vec{a}+\vec{a}\times \vec{b}=\vec{0} $$

note that

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{b}=-(\vec{b}\times \vec{a}) $$

So,

$$ \vec{r}\times \vec{a}=\vec{b}\times \vec{a} $$

Hence,

$$ (\vec{r}-\vec{b})\times \vec{a}=0 $$

This means $\vec{r}-\vec{b}$ is parallel to $\vec{a}$. Therefore,

$$ \vec{r}=\vec{b}+\lambda \vec{a} $$

for some scalar $\lambda$.

Now use the condition

$$ \vec{r}\cdot \vec{a}=0 $$

Substitute $\vec{r}=\vec{b}+\lambda \vec{a}$:

$$ (\vec{b}+\lambda \vec{a})\cdot \vec{a}=0 $$

So,

$$ \vec{b}\cdot \vec{a}+\lambda (\vec{a}\cdot \vec{a})=0 $$

Now calculate these dot products.

1. Find $\vec{b}\cdot \vec{a}$

$$ \vec{b}=(0,1,2), \qquad \vec{a}=(\sqrt{7},1,-1) $$

Thus,

$$ \vec{b}\cdot \vec{a}=0\cdot \sqrt{7}+1\cdot 1+2\cdot(-1)=1-2=-1 $$

2. Find $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{a}$

$$ \vec{a}\cdot \vec{a}=(\sqrt{7})^2+1^2+(-1)^2=7+1+1=9 $$

Therefore,

$$ -1+9\lambda=0 $$

So,

$$ \lambda=\frac{1}{9} $$

Hence,

$$ \vec{r}=\vec{b}+\frac{1}{9}\vec{a} $$

Now find $|\vec{r}|^2$.

Using

$$ \vec{r}=\vec{b}+\frac{1}{9}\vec{a} $$

we get

$$ |\vec{r}|^2=\left|\vec{b}+\frac{1}{9}\vec{a}\right|^2 $$

Expand:

$$ |\vec{r}|^2=|\vec{b}|^2+\frac{1}{81}|\vec{a}|^2+\frac{2}{9}(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}) $$

Now,

$$ |\vec{b}|^2=0^2+1^2+2^2=5 $$

$$ |\vec{a}|^2=9 $$

$$ \vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}=-1 $$

So,

$$ |\vec{r}|^2=5+\frac{9}{81}+\frac{2}{9}(-1) $$

$$ =5+\frac{1}{9}-\frac{2}{9} =5-\frac{1}{9} =\frac{44}{9} $$

Therefore,

$$ |3\vec{r}|^2=9|\vec{r}|^2=9\cdot \frac{44}{9}=44 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{44} $$

Hence, Option A.
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift

question: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$. Then the square of the area of the triangle with adjacent sides determined by the vectors $(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b})$ is :
solution:
Given

$$ \vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}, \qquad \vec{b} = 6\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k} $$

We need the square of the area of the triangle whose adjacent sides are

$$ 2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b} \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{a} - \vec{b} $$

For two adjacent sides $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$, area of triangle is

$$ \frac{1}{2} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}| $$

So, square of the area is

$$ \left( \frac{1}{2} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}| \right)^2 = \frac{1}{4} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}|^2 $$

Let

$$ \vec{u} = 2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b}, \qquad \vec{v} = \vec{a} - \vec{b} $$

First find $\vec{u}$:

$$ 2\vec{a} = 2(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k}) = 4\hat{i}+6\hat{j}+6\hat{k} $$

$$ 3\vec{b} = 3(6\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k}) = 18\hat{i}+9\hat{j}+9\hat{k} $$

Therefore,

$$ \vec{u} = 2\vec{a}+3\vec{b} = 22\hat{i}+15\hat{j}+15\hat{k} $$

Now find $\vec{v}$:

$$ \vec{v} = \vec{a}-\vec{b} = (2-6)\hat{i} + (3-3)\hat{j} + (3-3)\hat{k} = -4\hat{i} $$

Now compute $\vec{u} \times \vec{v}$:

$$ \vec{u} \times \vec{v} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 22 & 15 & 15 \\ -4 & 0 & 0 \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding,

$$ \vec{u} \times \vec{v} = \hat{i}(15\cdot 0 - 15\cdot 0) - \hat{j}(22\cdot 0 - 15\cdot (-4)) + \hat{k}(22\cdot 0 - 15\cdot (-4)) $$

$$ = 0\hat{i} - 60\hat{j} + 60\hat{k} $$

So,

$$ |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}|^2 = 0^2 + (-60)^2 + 60^2 = 3600 + 3600 = 7200 $$

Hence square of the area of the triangle is

$$ \frac{1}{4} \times 7200 = 1800 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{1800} $$

So, Option C is correct.
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift

question: If $\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $\vec{c}$ be three vectors such that $\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{b}$ and $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=3$, then $\vec{c} \cdot(\vec{a}-2 \vec{b})$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
Let

$$ \vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \qquad \vec{b}=\hat{j}-\hat{k} $$

We have to find

$$ \vec{c}\cdot (\vec{a}-2\vec{b}) $$

given that

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{c}=\vec{b} \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{a}\cdot \vec{c}=3 $$

Let

$$ \vec{c}=x\hat{i}+y\hat{j}+z\hat{k} $$

Then

$$ \vec{a}=(1,1,1), \qquad \vec{b}=(0,1,-1) $$

Now compute $\vec{a}\times \vec{c}$:

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{c} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k}\\ 1 & 1 & 1\\ x & y & z \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding,

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{c} = \hat{i}(z-y)-\hat{j}(z-x)+\hat{k}(y-x) $$

Since this is equal to $\vec{b}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}$, we compare components:

$$ z-y=0 $$

$$ -(z-x)=1 \Rightarrow x-z=1 $$

$$ y-x=-1 $$

Also, from $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{c}=3$,

$$ x+y+z=3 $$

Now solve the equations.

From $z-y=0$,

$$ z=y $$

From $x-z=1$,

$$ x=y+1 $$

Substitute into $x+y+z=3$:

$$ (y+1)+y+y=3 $$

$$ 3y+1=3 $$

$$ 3y=2 \Rightarrow y=\frac{2}{3} $$

So,

$$ z=\frac{2}{3}, \qquad x=\frac{5}{3} $$

Hence,

$$ \vec{c}=\frac{5}{3}\hat{i}+\frac{2}{3}\hat{j}+\frac{2}{3}\hat{k} $$

Now find $\vec{a}-2\vec{b}$:

$$ \vec{a}-2\vec{b} = (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})-2(\hat{j}-\hat{k}) $$

$$ = \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3\hat{k} $$

Therefore,

$$ \vec{c}\cdot(\vec{a}-2\vec{b}) = \left(\frac{5}{3},\frac{2}{3},\frac{2}{3}\right)\cdot(1,-1,3) $$

$$ = \frac{5}{3}-\frac{2}{3}+2 $$

$$ = \frac{3}{3}+2=1+2=3 $$

So, the required value is

$$ \boxed{3} $$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift

question:
Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}=10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and a vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ be such that $2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})+3(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=\overrightarrow{0}$.

If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=15$, then $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$ is equal to :
solution:
The unknown vector $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}$ is represented in 3D: $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}=\boldsymbol{x} \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\boldsymbol{y} \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+z \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$

Cross product of 2 vectors $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}$ :

$$ (\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}})=\left|\begin{array}{l|r|r} \hat{\boldsymbol{i}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{j}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \tag{i}\\ 4 & -1 & 3 \\ 10 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+34 \hat{j}+18 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \ldots . . (i)$$

Cross product of 2 vectors $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}$ :

$$ (\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}})=\left|\begin{array}{l|l|r} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 10 & 2 & -1 \\ \boldsymbol{x} & \boldsymbol{y} & \boldsymbol{z} \end{array}\right|=(2 z+y) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-(10 z+\boldsymbol{x}) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(10 \boldsymbol{y}-2 \boldsymbol{x}) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i}) $$

We know, $2(\boldsymbol{a} \times \boldsymbol{b})+3(\boldsymbol{b} \times \boldsymbol{c})=0$

$\Rightarrow 2(-5 \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+34 \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+18 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}})+3\{(2 \boldsymbol{z}+\boldsymbol{y}) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-(10 \boldsymbol{z}+x) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(10 \boldsymbol{y}-2 x) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}\}=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $(6 z+3 y-10) \hat{i}+(68-30 z-3 x) j+(36+30 y-6 x) \hat{k}=0$

As individual vector components of a null vector are zero.

$\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{x}-5 \boldsymbol{y} & =6 \ldots . \boldsymbol{P}_1 \\ \boldsymbol{y}+2 \boldsymbol{z} & =\frac{10}{3} \ldots . . \boldsymbol{P}_2 \\ 10 \boldsymbol{z}+\boldsymbol{x} & =\frac{68}{3} \ldots . \boldsymbol{P}_3\end{aligned}$

$(\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{c})=15 \Rightarrow(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k})=4 x-y+3 z=15 \ldots \ldots(i v)$

Equation set $(i i i)$ is mathematically constructed to form a linear combination of 2 planes $\boldsymbol{P}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{P}_2$ generating the $3^{\text {rd }}$ plane $\boldsymbol{P}_3$ :

$$ \boldsymbol{x}-5 \boldsymbol{y}+(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \cdot(\boldsymbol{y}+2 \boldsymbol{z})=\frac{68}{3} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\lambda}=5 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{P}_1+5 \boldsymbol{P}_2=\boldsymbol{P}_3 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{v}) $$

Equation ( $\boldsymbol{v}$ ) is conclusive in generating a family of planes $\left\{\boldsymbol{P}_1, \boldsymbol{P}_2, \boldsymbol{P}_3\right\}$ indicating infinite solutions for the system equation (iii)

We need an auxiliary equation provided through (iv) to render an unique solution for the vector $\vec{c}$ :

$\left[\begin{array}{rlrr}x-5 y & =6 & \ldots . & P_1 \\ y+2 z & =\frac{10}{3} & \ldots . & P_2 \\ 4 x-y+3 z & =15 & \ldots . & P_4\end{array}\right] \ldots .(v i)$

Equation (vi) can be solved using Determinants and Cramer's rule :

$$ \boldsymbol{\Delta}=\left|\begin{array}{r|r|r} 1 & -5 & 0 \tag{vii}\\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \end{array}\right| \quad \leftarrow \quad \boldsymbol{R}_3=\boldsymbol{R}_3-4 \boldsymbol{R}_1=\left|\begin{array}{r|r|r} 1 & -5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 19 & 3 \end{array}\right|=-35 \quad \ldots . . (vii)$$

$\Delta_x=\left|\begin{array}{|l|r|l|}6 & -5 & 0 \\ \frac{10}{3} & 1 & 2 \\ 15 & -1 & 3\end{array}\right|$

$\begin{aligned} & \boldsymbol{R}_1=\boldsymbol{R}_1+5 \boldsymbol{R}_2 \\ & \boldsymbol{R}_3=\boldsymbol{R}_3+\boldsymbol{R}_2\end{aligned}$

$=\left|\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\frac{68}{3} & 0 & 10 \\ \frac{10}{3} & 1 & 2 \\ \frac{55}{3} & 0 & 5\end{array}\right|$

$C_1 \leftrightarrows C_2$

$=-\left|\begin{array}{l|l}0 & \frac{68}{3} \\ 1 & \frac{10}{3} \\ 0 & \frac{10}{3} \\ \frac{55}{3}\end{array}\right|=\left(\frac{68}{3}\right) \cdot(5)-\left(\frac{55}{3}\right) \cdot(10)=-70 \ldots \ldots(v i i i)$

Equations $(\boldsymbol{v i i}),(\boldsymbol{v i i i}),(\boldsymbol{i x})$ and $(\boldsymbol{x})$ provides the solution to system equation $(\boldsymbol{v i})$ from Cramer's rule :

$\left[\begin{array}{l}x=\frac{\Delta_x}{\Delta}=\frac{70}{35}= \\ y=\frac{\Delta_y}{\Delta}=-\frac{28}{35}=-\frac{4}{5} \\ z=\frac{\Delta_z}{\Delta}=\frac{217}{(3) \cdot(35)}=\frac{31}{15}\end{array}\right]$

$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}=(2) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\left(\frac{31}{15}\right) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$

$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}) \cdot(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) \\ & =\left\{(2) \hat{i}-\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \hat{j}+\left(\frac{31}{15}\right) \hat{k}\right\} \cdot(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) \\ & =2-\frac{4}{5}-\frac{31}{5}\end{aligned}$

$=-\frac{4+31-10}{5} =-5$
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 8th April Evening Shift

question: If $4\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k$ and $p\hat i + q\hat j - 2\hat k$ are collinear vectors, then what are the possible values of p and q respectively?
solution:
Collinear vectors are vectors that are scalar multiples of each other. Thus, for two vectors $ \vec{A} $ and $ \vec{B} $ to be collinear, there must exist a scalar $ \lambda $ such that:

$$ \vec{A} = \lambda \vec{B} $$

Given:

$$ \vec{A} = 4\hat{i} + \hat{j} - 3\hat{k} $$

$$ \vec{B} = p\hat{i} + q\hat{j} - 2\hat{k} $$

We can write the relationship between $ \vec{A} $ and $ \vec{B} $ as:

$$ 4\hat{i} + \hat{j} - 3\hat{k} = \lambda \left( p\hat{i} + q\hat{j} - 2\hat{k} \right) $$

Now, equating the coefficients of $ \hat{i} $, $ \hat{j} $, and $ \hat{k} $ from both sides, we get:

  • For the $ \hat{i} $ component: $ 4 = \lambda p $
  • For the $ \hat{j} $ component: $ 1 = \lambda q $
  • For the $ \hat{k} $ component: $ -3 = \lambda (-2) $

From the $ \hat{k} $ component equation:

$$ -3 = -2\lambda $$

$$ \lambda = \frac{3}{2} $$

Using $ \lambda = \frac{3}{2} $ in the $ \hat{i} $ component equation:

$$ 4 = \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) p $$

$$ p = \frac{8}{3} $$

Using $ \lambda = \frac{3}{2} $ in the $ \hat{j} $ component equation:

$$ 1 = \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) q $$

$$ q = \frac{2}{3} $$

Therefore, the values of $ p $ and $ q $ are $ \frac{8}{3} $ and $ \frac{2}{3} $, respectively. Hence, the correct option is:

Option C: $ \frac{8}{3}, \frac{2}{3} $

question: If $\vec a, \vec b, \vec c$, are the position vectors of the vertices A, B, C respectively of a triangle ABC and G is the centroid of the triangle, then what is $\overrightarrow{AG}$ equal to ? .
solution:
To solve for the vector $ \overrightarrow{AG} $, we need to understand the properties of the centroid of a triangle. The centroid $ G $ of a triangle with vertices $ A, B, $ and $ C $ is the point where the three medians intersect. Importantly, the coordinates of the centroid $ G $ are given by the average of the coordinates of the vertices of the triangle.

The position vector of the centroid $ G $ is given by:

$$ \vec{G} = \frac{\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c}}{3} $$

Here, $ \vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c} $ are the position vectors of the vertices $ A, B, C $ respectively. Now, we need to find $ \overrightarrow{AG} $, which is the vector from point $ A $ to the centroid $ G $.

The vector $ \overrightarrow{AG} $ is given by:

$$ \overrightarrow{AG} = \vec{G} - \vec{a} $$

Substituting $ \vec{G} = \frac{\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c}}{3} $ into the equation above, we get:

$$ \overrightarrow{AG} = \frac{\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c}}{3} - \vec{a} $$

Combining the terms inside the fraction, we get:

$$ \overrightarrow{AG} = \frac{\vec{a} + \vec{b} + \vec{c} - 3\vec{a}}{3} $$

Simplifying further, this becomes:

$$ \overrightarrow{AG} = \frac{\vec{b} + \vec{c} - 2\vec{a}}{3} $$

Thus, the correct option is:

C: $\frac{\vec{b} + \vec{c} - 2\vec{a}}{3}$

question:
Consider the following statements :

1. Dot product over vector addition is distributive

2. Cross product over vector addition is distributive

3. Cross product of vectors is associative

Which of the above statements is/are correct ?
solution:
To determine which statements are correct, let’s analyze them one by one based on the properties of vector operations:

Statement 1: Dot product over vector addition is distributive

This statement asserts that the dot product is distributive with respect to vector addition. Mathematically, for vectors A, B, and C, this would be expressed as:

$$ \mathbf{A} \cdot (\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{C}) = (\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}) + (\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) $$

This is a well-known property of the dot product and is indeed true. Therefore, statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2: Cross product over vector addition is distributive

This statement claims that the cross product is distributive over vector addition. Mathematically, for vectors A, B, and C, it can be written as:

$$ \mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{C}) = (\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}) + (\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{C}) $$

The cross product is also distributive with respect to vector addition, which means statement 2 is correct.

Statement 3: Cross product of vectors is associative

This statement suggests that the cross product is associative. Mathematically, if this were true, we would have:

$$ (\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}) \times \mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) $$

However, the cross product of vectors is not associative. The left-hand side and the right-hand side of the above expression generally yield different results. Thus, statement 3 is incorrect.

Based on this analysis, the correct statements are 1 and 2.

Therefore, the correct answer is: Option C: 1 and 2 only.

question:
Let  $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ be three non-zero vectors such that  $\vec{a}\times \vec{b} = \vec{c} $. Consider the following statements:

1. $\vec a$ is unique if $\vec b$ and $\vec c$ are given

2. $\vec c$ is unique if $\vec a$ and $\vec b$ are given

Which of the above statements is/are correct?
solution:
To address the problem, let's analyze the given statements using vector algebra and properties of the cross product.

Statement 1: $\vec{a}$ is unique if $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ are given

Recall that the cross product of two vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ results in a vector $\vec{c}$ that is orthogonal to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$. The relationship is given by:

$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \vec{c}$$

This means that if $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ are known, $\vec{a}$ can lie in the plane orthogonal to both vectors and is not unique. Therefore, Statement 1 is incorrect.

Statement 2: $\vec{c}$ is unique if $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are given

By the definition of the cross product, if two vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are given, the resulting vector $\vec{c}$ is always uniquely determined and orthogonal to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$. Hence, Statement 2 is correct.

Based on the explanations, let's evaluate the options:

  • Option A: Statement 1 only - Incorrect
  • Option B: Statement 2 only - Correct
  • Option C: Both 1 and 2 - Incorrect
  • Option D: Neither 1 nor 2 - Incorrect

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option B: 2 only

question: Let $\vec a$ and $\vec b$ be two unit vectors such that $|\vec a - \vec b|<2.$ If 2θ is the angle between $\vec a$ and $\vec b,$ then which one of the following is correct ?
solution:
Let's start by analyzing the given conditions. We have two unit vectors, $$\vec{a}$$ and $$\vec{b}$$, such that:

  1. $$|\vec{a} - \vec{b}| < 2$$

  2. The angle between $$\vec{a}$$ and $$\vec{b}$$ is $$2\theta$$.

Given that $$\vec{a}$$ and $$\vec{b}$$ are unit vectors, the magnitude of each vector is 1. We can use the properties of vectors to express $$|\vec{a} - \vec{b}|$$ in terms of the angle between $$\vec{a}$$ and $$\vec{b}$$.

The magnitude of a difference of two vectors is given by:

$$|\vec{a} - \vec{b}| = \sqrt{(\vec{a})^2 + (\vec{b})^2 - 2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}$$

Since $$\vec{a}$$ and $$\vec{b}$$ are unit vectors:

$$|\vec{a}| = 1 \text{ and } |\vec{b}| = 1$$

This simplifies to:

$$|\vec{a} - \vec{b}| = \sqrt{1 + 1 - 2 \cos(2\theta)}$$

Simplify further:

$$|\vec{a} - \vec{b}| = \sqrt{2 - 2 \cos(2\theta)} = \sqrt{2(1 - \cos(2\theta))}$$

Given that $$|\vec{a} - \vec{b}| < 2$$, we have:

$$\sqrt{2(1 - \cos(2\theta))} < 2$$

Square both sides:

$$2(1 - \cos(2\theta)) < 4$$

Divide by 2:

$$1 - \cos(2\theta) < 2$$

This simplifies to:

$$-\cos(2\theta) < 1$$

Since $$\cos(2\theta)$$ ranges from -1 to 1 for any angle $$\theta$$, this inequality is always true. So, there are no additional constraints from this part of the calculation.

We now need to determine the range for $$\sin(\theta)$$. We know that the double angle formula for the cosine function is:

$$\cos(2\theta) = 1 - 2\sin^2(\theta)$$

Plugging this into the earlier simplified inequality:

$$-\cos(2\theta) < 1$$ is equivalent to:

$$- (1 - 2\sin^2(\theta)) < 1$$

Simplify further:

$$-1 + 2\sin^2(\theta) < 1$$

Adding 1 to both sides:

$$2\sin^2(\theta) < 2$$

Divide by 2:

$$\sin^2(\theta) < 1$$

The range for $$\sin^2(\theta)$$ is from 0 to less than 1. Therefore, the possible range for $$\sin(\theta)$$ is:

$$-1 < \sin(\theta) < 1$$

This matches Option D. Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$-1 < \sin \theta < 1$$

question: A line makes angles α, β and γ with the positive directions of the coordinate axes. If $\vec{a}=(\sin^2 \alpha)\hat{i} + (\sin^2 \beta)\hat{j} + (\sin^2 \gamma)\hat{k} \text{ and } \vec{b} = \hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}$ , then what is $\vec{a}.\vec{b}$ equal to?
solution:
1. Let the direction cosines of the given line be

    $l = \cos\alpha,\;m = \cos\beta,\;n = \cos\gamma$.

    Then by definition

    $$l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1.$$

  2. Compute each $\sin^2$ in terms of direction cosines:

    $$\sin^2\alpha = 1 - \cos^2\alpha = 1 - l^2,$$

    $$\sin^2\beta = 1 - \cos^2\beta = 1 - m^2,$$

    $$\sin^2\gamma = 1 - \cos^2\gamma = 1 - n^2.$$

  3. Given $\vec a = (\sin^2\alpha)\,\hat i + (\sin^2\beta)\,\hat j + (\sin^2\gamma)\,\hat k$ and $\vec b = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k$, their dot product is

    $$\vec a \cdot \vec b = \sin^2\alpha + \sin^2\beta + \sin^2\gamma.$$

  4. Substitute the expressions from step 2:

    $$\vec a \cdot \vec b = (1 - l^2) + (1 - m^2) + (1 - n^2) = 3 - (l^2 + m^2 + n^2).$$

  5. Using $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$, we get

    $$\vec a \cdot \vec b = 3 - 1 = 2.$$

Answer: 2. (Option D)

question:
Consider the following statements with respect to a vector d = (a × b) × c:

I. d is coplanar with a and b.

II. d is perpendicular to c.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
solution:
1. Use the triple-product identity

    $$(\mathbf{u}\times \mathbf{v})\times \mathbf{w} =\mathbf{v}\,(\mathbf{u}\cdot \mathbf{w}) -\mathbf{u}\,(\mathbf{v}\cdot \mathbf{w}).$$

  2. Let $\mathbf{u}=a,\;\mathbf{v}=b,\;\mathbf{w}=c$. Then

    $$d=(a\times b)\times c =b\,(a\cdot c)\;-\;a\,(b\cdot c).\tag{1}$$

  3. From (1) $d$ is a linear combination of $a$ and $b$.

    ⇒ $d$ lies in the plane of $a$ and $b$.

    ⇒ Statement I is correct.

  4. Compute $d\cdot c$:

    $$d\cdot c =\bigl[b\,(a\cdot c)-a\,(b\cdot c)\bigr]\cdot c =(a\cdot c)(b\cdot c)\;-\;(b\cdot c)(a\cdot c) =0.$$

    ⇒ $d\perp c$.

    ⇒ Statement II is correct.

Answer: Both I and II. (Option C)

question: The position vectors of three points A, B and C are a, b and c respectively such that $\vec{3a}-\vec{4b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0}$ What is AB:BC equal to?
solution:
1. The equation $3\mathbf a -4\mathbf b +\mathbf c= \mathbf 0$ is given.

    We can move terms to find $\mathbf c$:

    $$\mathbf c = -3\mathbf a + 4\mathbf b$$

  2. Now, let's find the vector $\overrightarrow{BC}$:

    $\overrightarrow{BC} = \mathbf c - \mathbf b$

    Substitute the value of $\mathbf c$:

    $\overrightarrow{BC} = (-3\mathbf a + 4\mathbf b) - \mathbf b$

    $= -3\mathbf a + 3\mathbf b$

    $= 3(\mathbf b - \mathbf a)$

    Notice that $\mathbf b - \mathbf a = \overrightarrow{AB}$, so:

    $\overrightarrow{BC} = 3\overrightarrow{AB}$

  3. This means the length of $BC$ is three times the length of $AB$:

    $|\overrightarrow{BC}| = 3|\overrightarrow{AB}|$

    So the ratio is:

    $|\overrightarrow{AB}| : |\overrightarrow{BC}| = 1:3$

Answer: 1 : 3.

question: The position vectors of three points A, B and C respectively, where  $\vec{a} ,\vec{b} $ and $\vec{c} $  respectively, where $\vec{c} = (\cos^2 \theta)\vec{a}+(\sin^2 \theta)\vec{b}$. What is $(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) + (\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) + (\vec{c} \times \vec{a})$ equal to?
solution:
1. Write $\vec c$ as a linear combination of $\vec a$ and $\vec b$

    Let

    $$\vec c=\cos^2\theta\,\vec a+\sin^2\theta\,\vec b\,,\quad\text{so set }\alpha=\cos^2\theta,\;\beta=\sin^2\theta.$$

  2. Compute each cross‐product in terms of $\vec a\times\vec b$

    a) $\vec a\times\vec b$ remains as is.

    b) $\vec b\times\vec c =\vec b\times(\alpha\vec a+\beta\vec b) =\alpha\,\vec b\times\vec a+\beta\,\vec b\times\vec b =\alpha(-\,\vec a\times\vec b)+0 =-\alpha\,(\vec a\times\vec b).$

    c) $\vec c\times\vec a =(\alpha\vec a+\beta\vec b)\times\vec a =\alpha\,\vec a\times\vec a+\beta\,\vec b\times\vec a =0+\beta(-\,\vec a\times\vec b) =-\beta\,(\vec a\times\vec b).$

  3. Sum them:

    $$(\vec a\times\vec b)+(\vec b\times\vec c)+(\vec c\times\vec a) =(\vec a\times\vec b)\;-\;\alpha\,(\vec a\times\vec b)\;-\;\beta\,(\vec a\times\vec b)$$

    Factor out $(\vec a\times\vec b)$:

    $$=\bigl[1-\alpha-\beta\bigr]\,(\vec a\times\vec b) =\bigl[1-(\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta)\bigr]\,(\vec a\times\vec b) =0\cdot(\vec a\times\vec b) =\vec 0.$$

Answer: $\boxed{\vec 0}$ (Option A).

question: Let $\vec{a},\vec{b} ,(\vec{a}\times\vec{b})$ be unit vectors. What is $\vec{a}.\vec{b}$ equal to?
solution:
1. Given Information

    The lengths of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are both 1. That means they are unit vectors: $\bigl\|\vec a\bigr\|=1$ and $\bigl\|\vec b\bigr\|=1$.

    The length of $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ is also 1, so $\bigl\|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}\bigr\| = 1$.

  2. Formula for Cross Product

    The length of the cross product is given by:

    $$\bigl\|\vec a\times\vec b\bigr\| = \|\vec a\|\, \|\vec b\|\, \sin\theta$$

    Here, $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.

  3. Find the Angle $\theta$

    Plug in the values we know:

    $$1 = 1 \times 1 \times \sin\theta$$

    This means $\sin\theta = 1$.

    So, $\theta = 90^\circ$. The vectors are at a right angle to each other.

  4. Dot Product

    The dot product uses the formula:

    $$\vec a\cdot\vec b = \|\vec a\|\, \|\vec b\|\, \cos\theta$$

    Substitute our values:

    $$1 \times 1 \times \cos 90^\circ = 0$$

The answer is 0 (Option A).

question:
Let $\rm \vec{a}, \vec{b}$ and $\rm\vec{c}$ be unit vectors such that $\rm\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{c}$. If θ is the angle between $\rm\vec{a}$ and $\rm\vec{b}$, then which of the following is/are correct?

1. $\rm\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = sin ~\theta~ \vec{c} $

2. $\rm\vec {a} \cdot (\vec{b}\times \vec{c})=0$

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
solution:
Given:

$|\vec{a}| = |\vec{b}| = |\vec{c}| = 1$ (All are unit vectors)

$\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{c}$.

Concept:

For two vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, their cross product is given by:

$ \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = |\vec{a}| |\vec{b}| \sin \theta \, \hat{n} $

where $\hat{n}$ is the unit vector indicating the direction of $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$. The direction of $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ is perpendicular to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.

Scalar Triple Product:

$ [\overrightarrow{a} \, \overrightarrow{b} \, \overrightarrow{c}] = \vec{a} \cdot (\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) $

If any two vectors among $\vec{a}$, $\vec{b}$, and $\vec{c}$ are equal, the scalar triple product is zero.

Explanation:

Statement 1:

The cross product $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ is:

$ \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = |\vec{a}| |\vec{b}| \sin \theta \, \hat{n} $

Since $\hat{n}$ is a unit vector in the direction of $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$, statement 1 stating $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \sin \theta \, \vec{c}$ is incorrect. This is because $\vec{c}$ and $\hat{n}$ are perpendicular to each other.

Hence, statement 1 is incorrect.

Statement 2:

Given the property of the scalar triple product:

$ [\vec{a} \, \vec{b} \, \vec{c}] = \vec{c} \cdot (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) $

Since $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ are perpendicular, we have:

$ \vec{c} \cdot (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) = 0 $

Thus, $\vec{a} \cdot (\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) = 0$.

So, statement 2 is correct.

Therefore, statement 2 is the correct option.

question: If $\rm\vec{a}+3\vec{b} = 3\hat{i}- \hat{j}$ and $\rm2\vec{a}+\vec{b} = \hat{i}- 2\hat{j}$, then what is the angle between $\rm\vec{a}$ and $\rm\vec{b}$
solution:
Concept:

If θ is the angle between vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, then

$ \cos \theta = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}| |\vec{b}|} $

Calculation:

Let θ be the angle between vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.

Given:

$ \vec{a} + 3\vec{b} = 3\hat{i} - \hat{j} \quad \text{....(i)} $

$ 2\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \hat{i} - 2\hat{j} \quad \text{....(ii)} $

To find $\vec{b}$, multiply equation (i) by 2 and then subtract equation (ii):

$ 2(\vec{a} + 3\vec{b}) - (2\vec{a} + \vec{b}) = 2(3\hat{i} - \hat{j}) - (\hat{i} - 2\hat{j}) $

$ 2\vec{a} + 6\vec{b} - 2\vec{a} - \vec{b} = 6\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} - \hat{i} + 2\hat{j} $

$ 5\vec{b} = 5\hat{i} $

$ \vec{b} = \hat{i} $

Substituting $\vec{b} = \hat{i}$ back into equation (i), we find:

$ \vec{a} + 3\hat{i} = 3\hat{i} - \hat{j} $

$ \vec{a} = -\hat{j} $

Now, calculate the dot product $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}$:

$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = (-\hat{j}) \cdot \hat{i} = 0 $

Using the concept:

$ \cos \theta = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}| |\vec{b}|} = 0 $

$ \theta = \cos^{-1}(0) $

$ \theta = \frac{\pi}{2} $

∴ The angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ is $\frac{\pi}{2}$.

question: If $\rm(\vec {a} + \vec{b})$ is perpendicular to $\rm\vec {a}$ and magnitude of $\rm\vec {b}$ is twice that of $\rm\vec {a}$, then what is the value of $\rm(4\vec {a} + \vec{b})\cdot \vec{b}$ equal to?
solution:
Concept:

If $(\vec{a} + \vec{b})$ is perpendicular to $\vec{a}$, then:

$ (\vec{a} + \vec{b}) \cdot \vec{a} = 0 $

Calculation:

From the equation above:

$ |\vec{a}|^2 + \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0 $

Given that the magnitude of $\vec{b}$ is twice that of $\vec{a}$:

$ |\vec{b}| = 2 \times |\vec{a}| $

This can be written as:

$ \frac{|\vec{b}|^2}{4} + \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0 $

Solving for $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}$:

$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = -\frac{|\vec{b}|^2}{4} \quad \text{-----(1)} $

To find $(4\vec{a} + \vec{b}) \cdot \vec{b}$:

$ (4\vec{a} + \vec{b}) \cdot \vec{b} = 4(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}) + |\vec{b}|^2 \quad \text{----(2)} $

Substituting equation (1) into equation (2):

$ (4\vec{a} + \vec{b}) \cdot \vec{b} = 4\left(-\frac{|\vec{b}|^2}{4}\right) + |\vec{b}|^2 = -|\vec{b}|^2 + |\vec{b}|^2 = 0 $

∴ The value of $(4\vec {a} + \vec{b})\cdot \vec{b}$ is equal to 0.

question:
Let $\rm\vec {a}, \vec{b}$ and $ \rm \vec {c}$ be three vectors such that  $\rm\vec {a}, \vec{b}$ and $ \rm \vec {c}$  are co-planar. Which of the following is/are correct?

1. $\rm(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c}$ is co-planar with $\rm\vec {a}$ and $\rm\vec {b}$

2. $\rm(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c}$ is perpendicular to $\rm\vec {a}$ and $\rm\vec {b}$

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
solution:
Concept:

If vectors $\rm\vec {a}, \rm\vec {b}$, and $\rm\vec {c}$ are co-planar, then:

$ \rm \vec{a} \cdot (\rm \vec{b} \times \rm \vec{c}) = 0 $

The vector $\rm \vec{a} \times \rm \vec{b}$ is perpendicular to both $\rm\vec {a}$ and $\rm\vec {b}$.

Calculation:

Let's consider:

$ \rm(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c} = \rm\vec {d} \quad (\text{Let}) $

$\rm\vec {d}$ is perpendicular to both $\rm \vec{a} \times \rm \vec{b}$ and $\rm \vec {c}$.

$\rm \vec{a} \times \rm \vec{b}$ is perpendicular to $\rm\vec {a}$ and $\rm\vec {b}$.

Therefore, $\rm\vec {d}$ or $\rm(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c}$ is co-planar with vectors $\rm\vec {a}$ and $\rm\vec {b}$.

Conclusion:

Statement 1 is correct because $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c}$ is co-planar with $\rm\vec {a}$ and $\rm\vec {b}$.

Statement 2 is incorrect; for statement 2 to be true, vector $\rm\vec {d}$ must be parallel or anti-parallel to $\rm \vec{a}\times\rm \vec{b}$, which contradicts the explanation for statement 1.

Thus, the best choice is option 1.

question: If the position vectors of A and B are (√2 - 1)î - ĵ and î + (√2 + 1)ĵ respectively, then what is the magnitude of $\rm \vec{AB}$?
solution:
Concept:

If $ A $ and $ B $ are points with position vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ respectively, then $\vec{AB} = \vec{b} - \vec{a}$.

If $\vec{a} = a_1\hat{i} + a_2\hat{j} + a_3\hat{k}$ is a vector, then the magnitude of the vector is given by $|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2}$.

Calculation:

Given:

Position vector of $ A = (\sqrt{2} - 1) \hat{i} - \hat{j} $

Position vector of $ B = \hat{i} + (\sqrt{2} + 1) \hat{j} $

To find $\vec{AB}$:

$ \vec{AB} = \vec{b} - \vec{a} = [\hat{i} + (\sqrt{2} + 1) \hat{j}] - [(\sqrt{2} - 1) \hat{i} + \hat{j}] $

Simplifying:

$ \vec{AB} = (-\sqrt{2} + 2) \hat{i} + (\sqrt{2} + 2) \hat{j} $

Now, calculate the magnitude of $\vec{AB}$:

$ |\vec{AB}| = \sqrt{(-\sqrt{2} + 2)^2 + (\sqrt{2} + 2)^2} $

Solving this:

$ |\vec{AB}| = \sqrt{((- \sqrt{2} + 2)^2) + ((\sqrt{2} + 2)^2)} = \sqrt{12} $

Thus:

$ |\vec{AB}| = 2\sqrt{3} $

Therefore, the magnitude of $\vec{AB}$ is $\mathbf{2\sqrt{3}}$.

question: Let $\vec{p}=\vec{a}-\vec{b},\vec{q}=\vec{a}+\vec{b}$. If $|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=2$ and $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}=2$, then what is the value of $|\vec{p}\times \vec{q}|$?
solution:
Given

$$ \vec{p}=\vec{a}-\vec{b}, \qquad \vec{q}=\vec{a}+\vec{b} $$

We need to find

$$ |\vec{p}\times \vec{q}| $$

Start by computing $\vec{p}\times \vec{q}$:

$$ \vec{p}\times \vec{q}=(\vec{a}-\vec{b})\times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) $$

Using distributive property of cross product,

$$ = \vec{a}\times \vec{a}+\vec{a}\times \vec{b}-\vec{b}\times \vec{a}-\vec{b}\times \vec{b} $$

Now,

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{a}=0, \qquad \vec{b}\times \vec{b}=0 $$

and

$$ \vec{b}\times \vec{a}= -\vec{a}\times \vec{b} $$

So,

$$ \vec{p}\times \vec{q}=\vec{a}\times \vec{b}-(-\vec{a}\times \vec{b}) =2(\vec{a}\times \vec{b}) $$

Therefore,

$$ |\vec{p}\times \vec{q}|=2|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}| $$

Now use the identity

$$ |\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{|\vec{a}|^2|\vec{b}|^2-(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b})^2} $$

Given

$$ |\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=2, \qquad \vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}=2 $$

So,

$$ |\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(2^2)(2^2)-2^2} =\sqrt{16-4} =\sqrt{12} =2\sqrt{3} $$

Hence,

$$ |\vec{p}\times \vec{q}|=2(2\sqrt{3})=4\sqrt{3} $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{4\sqrt{3}} $$

Option 4

question:
How many of the following can be a vector perpendicular to both the vectors $2\hat{i} - \hat{j} + \hat{k}$ and $\hat{i} + \hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$ ?

I. $4\hat{i}+5\hat{j}-3\hat{k}$

II. $-8\hat{i}-10\hat{j}+6\hat{k}$

III. $\frac{1}{50}(-4\hat{i}-5\hat{j}+3\hat{k})$

Select the correct answer.
solution:
Let the given vectors be

$$ \vec{a} = 2\hat{i} - \hat{j} + \hat{k} $$

and

$$ \vec{b} = \hat{i} + \hat{j} + 3\hat{k} $$

A vector perpendicular to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ must be parallel to their cross product $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$.

So, first find

$$ \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding:

$$ \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \hat{i}\left((-1)(3) - (1)(1)\right) -\hat{j}\left((2)(3) - (1)(1)\right) +\hat{k}\left((2)(1) - (-1)(1)\right) $$

$$ = \hat{i}(-3-1) - \hat{j}(6-1) + \hat{k}(2+1) $$

$$ = -4\hat{i} - 5\hat{j} + 3\hat{k} $$

So, any vector perpendicular to both must be of the form

$$ \lambda(-4\hat{i} - 5\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}) $$

for some scalar $\lambda$.

Now check the given vectors:

I. $4\hat{i} + 5\hat{j} - 3\hat{k}$

$$ 4\hat{i} + 5\hat{j} - 3\hat{k} = -1(-4\hat{i} - 5\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}) $$

So this is perpendicular to both.

II. $-8\hat{i} - 10\hat{j} + 6\hat{k}$

$$ -8\hat{i} - 10\hat{j} + 6\hat{k} = 2(-4\hat{i} - 5\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}) $$

So this is also perpendicular to both.

III. $\dfrac{1}{50}(-4\hat{i} - 5\hat{j} + 3\hat{k})$

This is clearly also a scalar multiple of $-4\hat{i} - 5\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$.

So this too is perpendicular to both.

Hence, all three vectors can be perpendicular to both given vectors.

$$ \boxed{\text{All three}} $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 4}} $$

question: What is the area of the parallelogram whose sides are represented by the vectors $\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+3\hat{k}$ and $2\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2\hat{k}$ ?
options: $\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{26} $ square units
solution:
Let the given vectors be

$$ \vec{a}=\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+3\hat{k} $$

and

$$ \vec{b}=2\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2\hat{k} $$

The area of the parallelogram formed by two vectors is given by the magnitude of their cross product:

$$ \text{Area}=|\vec{a}\times \vec{b}| $$

Now calculate $\vec{a}\times \vec{b}$:

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{b}= \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k}\\ 1 & 2 & 3\\ 2 & 1 & 2 \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding along the first row,

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{b} = \hat{i}(2\cdot 2-3\cdot 1)-\hat{j}(1\cdot 2-3\cdot 2)+\hat{k}(1\cdot 1-2\cdot 2) $$

$$ = \hat{i}(4-3)-\hat{j}(2-6)+\hat{k}(1-4) $$

$$ = \hat{i}-(-4)\hat{j}-3\hat{k} $$

$$ = \hat{i}+4\hat{j}-3\hat{k} $$

Now find its magnitude:

$$ |\vec{a}\times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{1^2+4^2+(-3)^2} $$

$$ =\sqrt{1+16+9} $$

$$ =\sqrt{26} $$

Hence, the area of the parallelogram is

$$ \boxed{\sqrt{26}\text{ square units}} $$

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 3}} $$

question:
The position vectors of the vertices A, B, Cand D of a quadrilateral ABCD are given by $3\hat{i}+4\hat{j}-2\hat{k}$ , $4\hat{i}-4\hat{j}-3\hat{k}$, $2\hat{i}-3\hat{j}+2\hat{k}$ and $6\hat{i}-2\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ respectively.
What is the angle between the diagonals AC and BD of the quadrilateral?
solution:
The diagonals of quadrilateral $ABCD$ are $AC$ and $BD$.

So first, let us find the vectors $\overrightarrow{AC}$ and $\overrightarrow{BD}$.

Given position vectors:

$$ \vec{A}=3\hat{i}+4\hat{j}-2\hat{k} $$

$$ \vec{B}=4\hat{i}-4\hat{j}-3\hat{k} $$

$$ \vec{C}=2\hat{i}-3\hat{j}+2\hat{k} $$

$$ \vec{D}=6\hat{i}-2\hat{j}+\hat{k} $$

Now,

$$ \overrightarrow{AC}=\vec{C}-\vec{A} $$

$$ = (2-3)\hat{i}+(-3-4)\hat{j}+\left(2-(-2)\right)\hat{k} $$

$$ = -\hat{i}-7\hat{j}+4\hat{k} $$

Also,

$$ \overrightarrow{BD}=\vec{D}-\vec{B} $$

$$ = (6-4)\hat{i}+(-2-(-4))\hat{j}+(1-(-3))\hat{k} $$

$$ = 2\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+4\hat{k} $$

Now use the formula for angle between two vectors:

$$ \cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{AC}\cdot \overrightarrow{BD}}{|\overrightarrow{AC}||\overrightarrow{BD}|} $$

First, find the dot product:

$$ \overrightarrow{AC}\cdot \overrightarrow{BD} = (-1)(2)+(-7)(2)+(4)(4) $$

$$ = -2-14+16=0 $$

So,

$$ \cos \theta=0 $$

Hence,

$$ \theta=90^\circ $$

Therefore, the angle between the diagonals $AC$ and $BD$ is

$$ \boxed{90^\circ} $$

So, the correct option is Option 1.

question: A force$\vec{F} = 2\hat{i} - \lambda\hat{j} + 5\hat{k}$ is applied at the point A(1, 2, 5) If its moment about the point B(- 1, - 2, 3) is $16\hat{i} - 6\hat{j} + 2\lambda\hat{k}$, then what is the value of $\lambda$ ?
solution:
The moment of a force about a point is given by

$$ \vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} $$

where $\vec{r}$ is the position vector from point $B$ to point $A$.

Given:

$$ \vec{F} = 2\hat{i} - \lambda \hat{j} + 5\hat{k} $$

Point $A(1,2,5)$ and point $B(-1,-2,3)$

So,

$$ \vec{r} = \overrightarrow{BA} = (1-(-1))\hat{i} + (2-(-2))\hat{j} + (5-3)\hat{k} $$

$$ \vec{r} = 2\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} + 2\hat{k} $$

Now calculate the cross product:

$$ \vec{M} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 4 & 2 \\ 2 & -\lambda & 5 \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding,

$$ \vec{M} = \hat{i}(4 \cdot 5 - 2(-\lambda)) - \hat{j}(2 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 2) + \hat{k}(2(-\lambda) - 4 \cdot 2) $$

$$ \vec{M} = \hat{i}(20 + 2\lambda) - \hat{j}(10 - 4) + \hat{k}(-2\lambda - 8) $$

$$ \vec{M} = (20+2\lambda)\hat{i} - 6\hat{j} + (-2\lambda-8)\hat{k} $$

Given moment is

$$ 16\hat{i} - 6\hat{j} + 2\lambda \hat{k} $$

Now compare corresponding components.

From $\hat{i}$ component:

$$ 20 + 2\lambda = 16 $$

$$ 2\lambda = -4 $$

$$ \lambda = -2 $$

Check with $\hat{k}$ component:

$$ -2\lambda - 8 = 2\lambda $$

$$ -8 = 4\lambda $$

$$ \lambda = -2 $$

So the correct value is

$$ \boxed{-2} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 1}} $$

question: What is $\vec{b}$ equal to ?
solution:
To find the vector $\vec{b}$ that satisfies the given conditions, we need to use both the dot product and the cross product properties provided.

Given:

$\vec{a} = 3 \hat{i} + 3 \hat{j} + 3 \hat{k}$

$\vec{c} = \hat{j} - \hat{k}$

$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 27$

$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \overrightarrow{9c} = 9 (\hat{j} - \hat{k}) = 9 \hat{j} - 9 \hat{k}$

Let us assume $\vec{b} = x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k}$

Step 1: Dot Product

Using the dot product property:

$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = (3\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}) \cdot (x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k})$

$\Rightarrow 3x + 3y + 3z = 27$

$\Rightarrow x + y + z = 9$ (Equation 1)

Step 2: Cross Product

Using the cross product property:

$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 3 & 3 \\ x & y & z \end{vmatrix}$

Calculating the determinant:

$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \hat{i} ( 3z - 3y ) - \hat{j} ( 3z - 3x ) + \hat{k} ( 3y - 3x )$

$\Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \hat{i} ( 3z - 3y ) - \hat{j} ( 3z - 3x ) + \hat{k} ( 3y - 3x ) = 9 \hat{j} - 9 \hat{k}$

Matching coefficients:

$\hat{i} : 3z - 3y = 0$ (Equation 2)

$\hat{j} : -( 3z - 3x ) = 9 \Rightarrow 3z - 3x = -9 \Rightarrow z - x = -3$ (Equation 3)

$\hat{k} : 3y - 3x = -9 \Rightarrow y - x = -3$ (Equation 4)

From Equation 3 and Equation 4:

$z = x - 3$ (Equation 3 rewritten)

$y = x - 3$ (Equation 4 rewritten)

Substitute $y$ and $z$ into Equation 1:

$x + (x - 3) + (x - 3) = 9$

$x + x - 3 + x - 3 = 9$

$3x - 6 = 9$

$3x = 15$

$x = 5$

Then:

$y = x - 3 = 5 - 3 = 2$

$z = x - 3 = 5 - 3 = 2$

Thus, $\vec{b} = 5 \hat{i} + 2 \hat{j} + 2 \hat{k}$

The correct option is Option B: $5 \hat{i} + 2 \hat{j} + 2 \hat{k}$

question: What is the angle between $(\vec{a}+\vec{b})$ and $\vec{c}$ ?
solution:
To determine the angle between the vectors $(\vec{a} + \vec{b})$ and $\vec{c}$, we need to first find the vector $\vec{b}$ from the given conditions. Let's begin by analyzing the provided information.

Given vectors:

$$\vec{a} = 3\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$$

$$\vec{c} = \hat{j} - \hat{k}$$

With the condition that:
